coredata.dc:title: Full color fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from triammonium citrate for cell imaging and white LEDs
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Qianyang
Zhou, Xianju
Xiang, Guotao
Jiang, Sha
Li, Li
Wang, Yongjie
Li, Yanhong
Cao, Zhongmin
Tang, Xiao
Ling, Faling
Luo, Xiaobing
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) exhibit prospective applications but the intrinsic reaction and luminescence mechanisms of CDs remain unclear. In this work, full color fluorescent CDs were synthesized through a facile solvothermal treatment with triammonium citrate as the carbon source. By adjusting the reaction temperature, the maximum emission wavelength is tuned from 440 nm for blue CDs (B-CDs) to 625 nm for red CDs (R-CDs), covering the whole visible spectrum range. The emission mechanism is proposed as surface-state-controlled, which results from different degrees of graphitization and increasing of functional groups on the surfaces. Three different surface states are derived from the overlap of the absorption and excitation spectra and the mechanism is established by XRD, Raman, XPS and UV–Vis absorption. The full-color luminescent CDs with high light and pH stability as well as good biocompatibility are successfully employed to image HeLa cells. White LED is fabricated by B-, G-, and R-CDs/polymer films with a near ultraviolet (NUV) semiconductor chip. The results of this research demonstrate that the synthesized CDs can be potentially applied in multi-color displays, white LEDs and biological imaging probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reaction temperature
CDs
Surface states
Multi-color emissive
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The applications of the CDs for cell imaging and WLEDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 11674044 , 11704054 ); Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing ( CSTC2017jcyjAX0046 , cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0525 , CSTC 2019jcyj-msxm1208 , KJZD-M202000601 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of preparation route of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs. (b) Optical images of 8 CDs samples in ethanol solution under 365 nm excitation in the dark box. (c) Corresponding PL emission spectra of these 8 samples.

Morphologies and structural characterization of the CDs. HRTEM images of CDs (a), B-CDs (b), G-CDs (c) and R-CDs (d). The inset in (b), (c) and (d) shows an enlarged HRTEM image of one CD with a scale bar of 2 nm. The size distribution of B-CDs (e), G-CDs (f) and R-CDs (g).
XRD patterns (a) and Raman spectra (b) of the three typical B-, G-, and R-CDs.
(a) The full survey of XPS of B/G/R-CDs. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of B-CDs (b), G-CDs (c), and R-CDs (d), respectively.

Photoluminescence B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b) and R-CDs (c) in ethanol as a function of excitation wavelengths. Excitation-emission matrix for B-CDs (d), G-CDs (e), and R-CDs (f). UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of B-CDs (g), G-CDs (h), and R-CDs (i).
Schematic diagram of synthesis (a) and luminescence (b) mechanism of B-, G-, and R-CDs. “G” in (b) means “ground state”.

The fluorescent image of HeLa cells labeled by CDs. Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with 30 mg mL−1 of B-, G-, and R-CDs for 6 h. (a–c) Bright-field, (d–f) overlay-field, (g-i/j-l) dark-field. All images were obtained under excitation at 408 nm.

(a) The photographs of three letters “AOC” under UV radiation, written by B-, G-, and R-CDs/PVA solution. (b) The pictures of B-, G-, and R-CDs/PVA films under daylight and (c) under 365 nm UV lamp. (d)Normalized emission spectra of the films under 375 nm excitation.

(a) CIE coordinates of the eight CDs/PVA composite films. (b)The photographs of the fabricated white LED. (c) The electroluminescence spectrum of the LED under the operating current of 60 mA. (d) The CIE coordinates of the packed working white LED.

head.ce:title: Full color fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from triammonium citrate for cell imaging and white LEDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Science, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Science, Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications, Chongqing, 400065, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing University of Posts and Telecommunications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) exhibit prospective applications but the intrinsic reaction and luminescence mechanisms of CDs remain unclear. In this work, full color fluorescent CDs were synthesized through a facile solvothermal treatment with triammonium citrate as the carbon source. By adjusting the reaction temperature, the maximum emission wavelength is tuned from 440 nm for blue CDs (B-CDs) to 625 nm for red CDs (R-CDs), covering the whole visible spectrum range. The emission mechanism is proposed as surface-state-controlled, which results from different degrees of graphitization and increasing of functional groups on the surfaces. Three different surface states are derived from the overlap of the absorption and excitation spectra and the mechanism is established by XRD, Raman, XPS and UV–Vis absorption. The full-color luminescent CDs with high light and pH stability as well as good biocompatibility are successfully employed to image HeLa cells. White LED is fabricated by B-, G-, and R-CDs/polymer films with a near ultraviolet (NUV) semiconductor chip. The results of this research demonstrate that the synthesized CDs can be potentially applied in multi-color displays, white LEDs and biological imaging probes.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs), which are defined as quasi-spheres with particle size less than 10 nm, are composed of carbon skeletons in core and functional groups on the surface. Compared with the traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), such as CdS QDs, fluorescent CDs have attracted increasing attention owing to their excellent properties such as low-toxicity, high biocompatibility, outstanding fluorescence stability, and splendid physiochemical characteristics [1–3].On the basis of their fantastic properties, CDs have been investigated for extensive applications, including biolabeling and cell imaging, lighting and displaying, catalyst and sensing [4–8]. CDs are considered to be preferable to some other fluorescent nanomaterials because of these distinct merits [9–11].

In the period of earlier studies, the synthesized CDs suffer from low photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) and the emission color of CDs is limited to blue. After more than one-decade, significant development has been made in this field. The QY of CDs reach up to 94% for blue [12], 60% for green [13], and 86% for red emission [6]. Full-color emissive CDs [4,8,11] as well as deep red emission with very narrow full width at half maximum [1] have been explored. However, due to the variety and complexity, the fundamental study related to CDs is still facing great challenges: the mechanism of the photoluminescence and the tunability of the emission colors of CDs along with the reason of the excitation-wavelength-dependence of the emission are controversial even contrary [14]. There are mainly three hypotheses which are proposed to clarify the emission mechanism of CDs. The first one is the size effect. Some research works convince that the size dependent quantum confinement effect accounts for the luminescence of CDs just as it does in the traditional quantum dots [15,16]. But other works disagree with this viewpoint because tunable emission is observed in CDs with similar particle size [17] or broad particle size distribution [18]. The second hypothesis is the surface-state controlled emission mechanism which is gradually gaining acceptance by more and more works [9,18–21]. But surface-state is determined by the morphology and the functional groups on the surface of CDs, which is complicated and difficult to be directly testified. The third one is molecular state emission mechanism, especially for CDs synthesized via bottom-up methods [2]. The intrinsic reaction and luminescence mechanisms of CDs are of great significance for the applications, therefore it requires thorough investigation.

The carbon source to prepare CDs includes natural materials (such as taxus leaves [1], glucose [22] and coffee [23]) and synthetic chemicals. Among these reagents, phenylenediamine [18,24,25] and citric acid [26–28] are most utilized. In this work, triammonium citrate, rather than citric acid, is employed as the carbon source based on the following considerations: 1) In many cases, the purification process of the obtained CDs in the reactions where citric acid is treated as the starting material, is tedious [26,29,30] and large amount of waste eluent liquor is produced. A more convenient and environment-friendly synthesis method is required. In this work, in the purification process of obtained CDs, only centrifuging, ultrasonic treatment, and freeze-drying are needed. Silica gel column and/or dialysis bag are not used, which simplifies the preparation procedure of CDs and decrease the amount of waste eluent liquors. 2) As compared with citric acid, triammonium citrate contains more nitrogen components, which might promote the nitrogen-based functional groups in CDs, leading to a more efficient emission-color tunability [30]. Herein, we used triammonium citrate (denoted as T-CA) and urea as starting materials, via a simple and cheap solvothermal reaction to develop a stable, highly emissive, color tunable emission CDs strategy. The CDs were successfully synthesized simply by adjusting the reaction temperature and the ratio of T-CA/urea. More interestingly, different emission color is obtained even from the samples with the same ratio of T-CA/urea but only varying the reaction temperatures (green sample 4 and orange sample 6). The morphologies, particle size distribution, chemical compositions, as well as the optical properties of the as-prepared CDs were investigated. The observation of the overlap of UV absorption and photoluminescence excitation spectra clearly confirms the presentence of the corresponding surface-states, which offers strong evidence for the emission mechanism of CDs. The prepared CDs were successfully utilized as in vitro imaging of HeLa cells and fabricate white LED with excellent performance.

Triammonium citrate (C6H17N3O7, T-CA), urea (H2NCONH2), ethanol (C2H5OH) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Chuandong Chemical Company. N, N-dimethylformamide (C3H7NO, DMF), ethylacetate (C4H8O2), petroleum ether (spectral purity) and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

A solvothermal method was employed to synthesize highly emissive CDs. To synthesize B-CDs, T-CA (0.2 g, 0.82 mmol) and urea (0.5 g, 8.33 mmol) was dissolved into 12 mL DMF solution and then the solution was kept at 150 °C for 14 h in a 30 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave. The CDs were collected by centrifuging the blue solution at 10000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant containing as-prepared CDs was washed by petroleum ether and ethyl acetate mixed solution (v:v = 3:1). Then the solution was shaken well and ultrasonic treated. The process was repeated several times to get B-CDs precipitation. A part of the precipitation was dispersed in ethanol to form colloidal solution. The rest of the precipitation was dissolved in water and followed by freeze-drying, which produced B-CDs powder. Eight CDs samples with different emission colors were synthesized through the same approach. The only difference for these samples is the reaction temperature and ratio of T-CA and urea. Table S1 in Supporting Information summarizes the detail information of the reaction conditions for these 8 samples. The obtained CDs show high dispersity in water and organic solvents.

PVA (2 g) powder was suspended in water (80 mL). PVA was easily dissolved absolutely with stirring and heating at 75 °C. Then CD sample was mixed with PVA aqueous solution, and the mixture was spread on a glass substrate and dried for 4 h at 45 °C. Thus, the CDs/PVA film was prepared.

HeLa cells were cultured in PRMI-1640 medium containing 10% PBS at 37 °C in 5% CO2. HeLa cells were inoculated on a fluorescent plate with about 1
×
105 cells per plate, cultured for 24 h and washed several times with PBS, then the CDs were added to the culture medium and cultivated for different time. The culture medium was removed and washed with PBS for 3 times. Finally, the fluorescent plate was placed in a confocal laser scanning microscope to shoot fluorescence imaging, and the fluorescence excitation channel was selected at 408 nm.

High Resolution Tunnelling Electron Microscope (HRTEM) investigation was obtained on a JEOL JEM-2100F instrument at 200 kV to study the sizes and morphologies of the CDs samples. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Cary 630 FT-IR spectrometer in the range of 4000–500 cm−1. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured on a Cary 5000 ultraviolet–visible–near-infrared spectrophotometer. Powder XRD was recorded by an XD-1 powder diffractometer using Cu
Kα
radiation (=1.5406 Å) at 36 kV and 20 mA. Raman analysis was performed by a Horiba France SAS confocal Raman microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out by an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with a monochromatic X-ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV). The excitation and emission spectra were recorded by an FLS920 spectrofluorometer. The absolute quantum yield was measured using a full functional steady-state and transient fluorescence spectrometer Horiba JobinYvon FluoroMax-4. To ensure the stability of the samples, they are kept at room temperature out of direct sunlight.

The highly emissive CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method, in which T-CA and urea were employed to react in DMF. Fig. 1 (a) schematically present the synthesis route of B-, G- and R-CDs. Fig. 1 (b) shows the optical images of eight CD samples under 365 nm UV light in the dark box. The difference among these samples in preparation is the ratio of T-CA/urea and the reaction temperature. The various emitting colors ranging from blue to red indicate that the emission of the synthesized CDs can be tuned by reaction conditions. The corresponding PL emission spectra under the excitation of 370 nm are shown in Fig. 1 (c), with the emission maxima at 440, 460, 480, 515, 550, 565, 600 and 625 nm respectively. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) for sample 1–8 is summarized in Table S1 as well. That is to say, the full visible range responsive carbon dots are successfully obtained by facile solvothermal treatment. The excitation-wavelength dependent emission is discussed in the latter section.

TEM and HRTEM have been employed to investigate the morphologies and the structural changes in B-, G- and R-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 2 (a), the nanostructures in CDs exist as quasi-spherical particles, some of which clearly show well-resolved lattice fringes and the others are amorphous. The former dots are labeled as “graphitic structure” while the latter ones are marked by “amorphous structure” in the figure. The coexistence of crystalline graphitic dots and amorphous structures is reported by previous works [31,32]. Figs. S1(a–f) in Supporting Information and Fig. 2(b–d) show the TEM images of the CDs. The inset HRTEM images show clear lattice fringes with interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, 0.28 nm and 0.32 nm for B-, G- and R-CDs respectively, which unveil that the CDs contain crystalline carbon structure since these lattice fringes are consistent with the (100) lattice spacing of graphene along the [001] direction [19,21,33,34]. The particle sizes distribution and average particle sizes of three CDs are presented in Fig. 2(e–g). The average particle sizes are 2.52, 3.44, and 3.59 nm for B-, G-, and R-CDs, respectively. It is seen that the size difference among these CDs is not obvious.

The crystallinity of three typical CDs is furthermore evaluated by XRD and Raman. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the XRD patterns exhibit a broad band centered at 2Θ = 21° as well as an overlapped minor sharp peak at 2Θ = 26.6°. The intensity of the sharp diffraction peak is enhancing in the order of B-, G- and R-CDs. Both the broad band and the sharp peak are attributed to (002) crystallographic plane of graphite, but the degree of crystalline related to the broad band is lower than that of the sharp peak. The wide peak at 2Θ = 21°, with d spacing of 0.40 nm, results from steric separation by functional groups or the plane distortion from sp3 C in the graphite plane. Whilst, the sharp peak at 26.6°, with d spacing of 0.35 nm, is the characteristic (002) diffraction peak of graphite. Its appearance indicates the interlayer stacking within CDs is very close to that in graphite, which illustrates that close packing of highly conjugate sp2 domain is predominant within CDs [20]. In addition, comparing the results of these three samples, the sharp peak at 2Θ = 26.6° emerges as the strongest one in R-CDs, which indicates that R-CDs has the highest degree of graphitization. The same conclusion is obtained by Raman results, as displayed in Fig. 3 (b). It is well known that the band D (1352 cm−1) and band G (1581 cm−1) in Raman spectra represent the vibrations of amorphous carbon and graphite carbon in CDs [34]. Correspondingly, the ID/IG ratio is the probe of the extent of crystalline graphite and the value of ID/IG reflects the ratio of sp3 /sp2 carbon. The ID/IG ratios of B-, G-, and R-CDs are 1.06, 0.98 and 0.92, respectively. The declined values reveal the increasing graphite content from B-, G to R-CDs, implying that R-CDs have the highest graphitization degree among these three samples, which is consistent with the results of XRD.

To further study the functional groups on CDs, we used different characterization techniques to investigate the chemical compositions of CDs. Fig. S2 in Supporting Information shows the FT-IR spectra of B-, G-, and R-CDs. The stretching vibration of –OH (3435 cm−1), –NH (3080 cm−1), –CH2 (2946 cm−1), CO (1665 cm−1), CN (1564 cm−1), CC (1480 cm−1) and C–O–C (1052 cm−1) are observed. Furthermore, the absorption intensity increasement of νC
O and δ C
N vibrations from B-CDs to R-CDs indicates a gradual enhancement in the amount of O- and N-containing functional groups. XPS shines a bright light on the chemical structure and composition of CDs. Fig. 4 (a) presents the full survey of XPS of B-, G-, and R-CDs, where the peaks contributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s are clearly recorded. The atomic composition of C, N and O is listed in Table S2. The ratio of N/C modifies from 0.2138, 0.2585 to 0.3218, which hints the nitrogen content is increasing from B-, to R-CDs. At the same time, the O/C ratio enhances from 0.2843, 0.3389 to 0.4521 for B-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively, revealing the boost of oxygen content.

Fig. 4(b–d) displays the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s for B-, G-, and R-CDs. It is reported that the XPS peak due to C1s can be divided four species: graphitic carbon (CC/C–C) locating at 284.1 eV, single-bonding carbon like C–O/C–N at 285.1 eV, carbonyl –CO at 287.1 eV, and carbon in carboxylic acid OC–OH at 288.5 eV, respectively [34]. The evaluated contents of four C species is tabulated in Table S3. Simultaneously, the ratio between sp3 (C–O/C–N) and sp2 (CC/C–C) depresses from 0.3787, 0.3603 to 0.3573 for B-, G-, and R-CDs, respectively, which confirms again the enhancement of graphitization degree in R-CDs as that found out in the XRD and Raman results. It is worthwhile to note that the atomic content ratio C:N:O of R-CDs in this work exhibits a much higher nitrogen and oxygen contents compared with the reported values which are obtained from the results originating from citric acid, as listed in Table S4 in the Supporting Information. It implies that triammonium citrate with higher nitrogen components might facilitate the functional groups produced in CDs. The high resolution XPS results of N1s and O1s due to B-, G-, and R-CDs are shown in Fig. S3 in Supporting Information. The XPS profile of N1s is fitted by three peaks corresponding to pyridinic N with the binding energy of 399.4 eV, pyrollic N of 400.5 eV, and graphitic N of 401.8 eV. Table S5 in Supporting Information points out that in B-CDs the predominant N exists as pyridinic N, while the dominant N in R-CDs is in the form of pyrollic N. Similarly, the XPS data of O1s containing two oxygen components including C–O at 534 eV and CO at 531.6 eV are investigated and the results are shown in Fig. S3 and Table S6. In this work, the content of functional groups containing O and N grows from B-CDs, G-CDs to R-CDs, which might lead to red shift of the fluorescence emission.

The luminescent properties of B-, G-, and R-CDs are studied. From Fig. 5 (a), it is seen that B-CDs demonstrate a blue emission covering a broad range from 400 to 600 nm upon UV light excitations. Furthermore, the emission of B-CDs shows a remarkable excitation-wavelength-dependent behavior, which demonstrates a red-shift of emission maximum with increasing of the excitation wavelength. The excitation of 360 nm is the most effective. As shown in Fig. 5(b) G-CDs show an intense green emission upon the excitations from 350 to 490 nm. The excitation-wavelength-dependent emission is not significant, with most of the emission maxima locating at 550 nm. However, unlike B-, and G-CDs, the spectrum shift is never observed for R-CDs within a wide range of excitation from 350 to 550 nm, indicating that R-CDs exhibit excitation independent emission, as shown in Fig. 5(c).

To further verify the observed excitation-dependent luminescence properties of the CDs, we carry out the excitation-emission matrix (EEM) investigation. As depicted in Fig. 5(d), the EEM of B-CDs covers a wide spectrum region and shows a strong dependence on excitation, which agrees well with Fig. 5 (a). On the contrary, the EEM of R-CDs presents a small range which indicates the emission is dominated solely by red emission under all excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 5 (f). The existence of multiple surface active sites [35] on individual CD particles and the mixing of CDs with different sizes [20,36] are the main hypotheses which are proposed to explain the excitation-wavelength dependent emission of CDs. Because HRTEM results have shown that there is no remarkable size difference in our prepared B-, G- and R-CDs, the multiple surface-active sites prefer to lead to the excitation-wavelength dependent emission. The values of absolute quantum yield (QY) for B-, G-, and R-CDs are estimated as 24.4%, 17.2% and 12.1% for B-, G- and R-CDs respectively (Please see Table S7 in Supporting Information). The luminescence decay curves and the corresponding lifetimes are displayed in Fig. S4 and Table S8 respectively. The average decay lifetimes are 11.4, 4.9 and 1.9 ns for G-, B- and R-CDs respectively, which are in agreement with the reported values [27,28].

Fig. 5 (a)–(f) emphasize the emission properties of these CDs, while the absorption and excitation spectra are presented in Fig. 5 (g)–(i). The B-CDs exhibit an intense absorption band centered at 350 nm (B3 in Fig. 5 (g)), which is overlapped with the excitation spectrum (pink curve) recorded by monitoring the emission at 440 nm. The absorption band at 350 nm in B-CDs is assigned to n→π* transitions of carbon cores [14,20,24]. For G-CDs, besides the aforementioned absorption band at 350 nm, another band is appeared at 470 nm (B2), which is original from the absorption of CO groups on the surface state of CDs [20,24,27,28]. It is interesting to note that this absorption band is well covered by the excitation spectrum monitoring the green emission, which indicates that the green emission is mainly caused by the transition of Band 2, rather than Band 3. In R-CDs, an additional obvious absorption band associated to the CN surface group [20,24,27,29] at 550 nm (B1), accompanied with the bands at 350 and 470 nm, is emerged. It is worthwhile to make it clear that only Band 1 is overlapped by the excitation spectrum monitoring the red emission at 625 nm. Similar with that occurs in G-CDs, it unveils the emission in R-CDs is primarily due to Band 1. The absorption spectra of B-, G-, and R-CDs are compiled in Fig. S5 in Supporting Information, in which the comparison of these three samples is easily made. As reported by previous literatures, the observed absorption bands at 350, 470 and 550 nm in this work, which are contributed to n
→
π* transitions of conjugated CO and CN bonds, are related to different surface-states [18–21,24]. These results clearly confirm that the surface-states account for the multicolor emission, with lower surface-state corresponding to longer wavelength emissions (green and red).

The controllable synthesis and reasonable luminescence mechanisms of the B-, G-, and R-CDs have been proposed. Fig. 6 (a) depicts the possible synthesis mechanism as a function of reaction temperature in this work. On the basis of chemical composition analysis of XPS (see Fig. 4 and S3, Tables S2–S5), it is known that the contents of N and O components increase in the order of B-, G- and R-CDs. Then, it is proposed that the higher reaction temperature accelerates the entrance of N and O components into the carbon dots, leading to the increase of functional groups in CDs. At the same time, the enhanced reaction temperature brings the higher degree of oxidation, which benefits to higher ordering and graphitization degree. It could be proved by XRD results, which demonstrate the increase of diffraction intensity due to (002) crystallographic plane at 2Θ = 26.6°. Raman data support this viewpoint as well by the decrease ID/IG ratio. In addition, T-CA with more nitrogen component might profit to the N-containing functional groups producing in CDs.

Fig. 6 (b) presents the luminescence mechanism of CDs. As discussed above, the higher reaction temperature enhances the content of functional groups, which produces new energy levels, called surface states here. It is similar as N-state in the work of N-doped red emissive carbon dot [5]. To some content, it is also analogous with the defect levels in inorganic phosphors. The surface-states in nature are caused by the functional groups attaching to the surface of CDs. Also, the surface state is affected by the morphology of CDs. The energy of the surface-states could be derived from the band edge of the absorption spectra of the samples [6]. There are three surface states S1, S2 and S3 in this work, which are derived from the absorption bands (B3, B2 and B1 correspondingly). With the increase functional groups on the surface and higher graphitization degree in carbon dots, the surface state with lower energy is activated and emits longer wavelength photons. In B-CDs, the functional groups result in the highest surface state S3 with the energy at about 30 K cm−1 and B-CDs absorb NUV light emit blue light. The increasing functional groups cause more surface states (S2 and S1) at lower energy, making G- and R-CDs absorb photons with low frequency and emit longer wavelength light. Thus, based on the proposed temperature-dependent synthesis and surface-state controlled luminescence mechanism, the green solvothermal reaction of T-CA based full-color emission CDs can be realized with this simple method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The applications of the CDs for cell imaging and WLEDs
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of CDs is very important for the applications. The photo-stability of the as-produced CD liquids with the concentration of 300 mg/L is testified by continuous radiation with a 365 nm UV lamp as shown in Fig. S6. The emission intensity maintains ~80% even after radiation for 11 h for B- and R-CDs. The G-CDs demonstrates a better photo-stability with more than 90% intensity after 11h radiation. The influence of pH values of the solution is investigated as displayed in Figs. S7–9 of Supporting Information. The results demonstrate that emission intensity varies and is highest in a pH neutral environment. But the emission color exhibits no obvious change. The possible reason might be that both acid functional groups (such as –COOH) and basic functional groups (NH2) are connected to CDs, which would act in basic or acid medium and decline the emission intensity.

Based on the excellent stability of the prepared CDs, they are employed to image HeLa cells. The cytotoxicity/biocompatibility toward HeLa cells is performed by the conventional MTT assay (Fig. S10 of Supporting Information). The result shows that after 24 h of incubation with (100–500 μg mL−1) B-, G-, and R-CDs, the cell viability is still over 85%. The cell imaging is investigated by laser scanning confocal microscopy. After treating HeLa cells with the CDs for 6 h in the fluorescence plate and washed with 10% PBS 3 times, the bright blue, green and red fluorescence was observed upon 408 nm excitation (Fig. 7 (d–l)). As shown in Fig. 7(a–c), the overlay images illustrate that the CDs are mainly situated in the cell membrane and cytoplasm, but have not entered into the nucleolus. Furthermore, the cells still keep good morphology, which hints good biocompatibility of the CDs. This phenomenon further confirms the advantages of the prepared B-, G-, and R-CDs for biolabeling.

The re-absorption of emitting light in solid state often results in extinguish of the emission of CDs, which is an obstacle for the applications [37,38]. To solve this problem, the CDs powders are mixed with PVA aqueous solution to get fluorescent inks or to prepare composite films, in which –COOH and/or –NH2 functional groups on the CDs surface will form hydrogen bonds with –OH in PVA molecules. It benefits the mono-dispersing of CDs particles in PVA network and prevents the agglomeration [37]. Fig. 8 (a) exhibits the photographs of three letters written by the ink containing CDs and PVA with high viscosity. They demonstrate bright fluorescence under UV excitation at room temperature.

Similarly, Fig. 8 (b) depicts the pictures of CDs/PVA films produced by the mixture after drying. The films have excellent transmission of light and shows great ductility. In addition, as shown in Fig. 8 (c), the films give rise to bright fluorescence of blue, green, and red emission when they are irradiated by UV light. The corresponding emission spectra of CDs/PVA films are shown in Fig. 8 (d). The spectra of the films are identical with the results of CDs in ethanol solutions. It indicates that these CDs/PVA composite films avoid self-quenching of photoluminescence. The above experiments unveil these CDs can be used as multicolor emitting ink and can form uniform composite films easily, which might find potential applications in the field of display.

The CIE coordinates calculated from the photoluminescence spectra of the 8 CDs/PVA composite films are shown in Fig. 9 (a). It is seen that the CIE coordinates of the films tune from blue to cyan, and then to green, yellow and finally to red region. When fabricating WLEDs, the CD/PVA composite film is prepared by mixing B-, G-, and R-CDs in a weight ratio of 1:1.5:2.5 into 2 ml PVA solution. Then, the prepared film is fixed on a 365 nm UV semiconductor chip with AB glue, and finally dried at 60 °C. The photographs of the obtained LED are depicted in Fig. 9 (b). The electro-fluorescence spectrum of the white LED under the operating current of 60 mA is shown in Fig. 9 (c). The obvious blue, green and red emission bands are clearly emerged in the spectrum. The CIE coordinates are (0.3605, 0.3531), as displayed in Fig. 9 (d), and the correlated color temperature Tc = 4421 K, with the color rendering index Ra of 83.4. The luminous efficiency of the fabricated WLED is 6.25 lm/W under the operating current of 60 mA. It indicates that the properties of the fabricated white LED are fine.

In summary, multicolor fluorescent CDs with low toxicity and high stability are successfully synthesized from cheap and readily available triammonium citrate (T-CA) and urea by solvothermal method. The emission color of the CDs can be tuned from blue to red, covering the entire visible region by adjusting the reaction temperature. The emission mechanism of the prepared CDs is proposed as surface-state-controlled, which is resulted from different degrees of graphitization and increasing content of functional groups on the surfaces. Three different surface states are directly derived from the absorption spectra of CDs and the mechanism is established by XRD, Raman, XPS and UV–Vis absorption. Triammonium citrate with more nitrogen content compared to citric acid benefits the producing nitrogen-containing functional groups. The CDs are successfully used to image HeLa cells. The as-prepared CDs are dissolved in polar organic solvents, forming a uniform solution and maintaining strong light emission. It is further demonstrated that CDs/polymer composite films can be easily formed using these CDs solutions. White LED is fabricated by the films and the CIE coordinates of the white LED are (0.3605, 0.3531) and the corresponding Tc = 4421 K. The results of this research illustrate that the obtained CDs are potentially applied in multi-color display, white LEDs and biological imaging probes.

Qianyang Chang: Writing – original draft, carried out most of the experiments and wrote the paper. Xianju Zhou: Supervision, the corresponding author, supervised all the work and revised the manuscript. Guotao Xiang: studied the excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Sha Jiang: Formal analysis, is responsible for the elemental analysis of C, H, N, O. Li Li: did the work of XPS and polished the paper. Yongjie Wang: Formal analysis, analyzed the results of FTIR. Yanhong Li: is responsible for HRTEM. Zhongmin Cao: Formal analysis, analyzed the stability of the samples. Xiao Tang: carried out the luminescence decay part. Faling Ling: took part in the mechanism of reaction. Xiaobing Luo: summarized Table S2-3.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (11674044, 11704054); Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTC2017jcyjAX0046, cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0525, CSTC 2019jcyj-msxm1208, KJZD-M202000601).
